title: Tight hardness of the non-commutative Grothendieck problem
speaker: Oded Regev
abstract: We prove that for any $\epsilon > 0$ it is NP-hard to approximate the non-commutative Grothendieck problem to within a factor $1/2 + \epsilon$, which matches the approximation ratio of the algorithm of Naor, Regev, and Vidick (STOC'13). Our proof uses an embedding of $\ell_2$ into the space of matrices endowed with the trace norm with the property that the image of standard basis vectors is longer than that of unit vectors with no large coordinates. The talk does not assume familiarity with the Grothendieck problem.

proof_stages.label: Classical Grothendieck inequality as an SDP relaxation
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls the classical Grothendieck optimization problem, its SDP relaxation by replacing signs with unit vectors, Grothendieck's constant-factor inequality, and the algorithmic rounding interpretation.

proof_stages.label: Noncommutative Grothendieck formulation and factor-two algorithm
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines the noncommutative version with bilinear forms on unitary matrices, describes the corresponding SDP and Hagerup's factor-two inequality, and explains why the resulting problem is computationally meaningful.

proof_stages.label: Tight hardness target via little noncommutative Grothendieck
proof_stages.summary: After reviewing the known hardness landscape for the commutative case, the main theorem is stated: approximating noncommutative Grothendieck within 2 minus epsilon is NP-hard, reduced to proving square-root-two hardness for the little noncommutative problem.

proof_stages.label: Little Grothendieck warm-up through operator-norm duality
proof_stages.summary: As a commutative warm-up, little Grothendieck is identified with the L2-to-L1 operator norm, then rewritten by duality and a positive-semidefinite factorization as a special case of the full Grothendieck problem.

proof_stages.label: PCP-to-geometric embedding hardness criterion
proof_stages.summary: The complexity-theoretic machinery is compressed into a geometric requirement: construct a linear map that gives standard basis directions norm one while forcing all sufficiently smooth vectors to have smaller norm, with constants sqrt(2/pi) in L1 and 1/sqrt(2) in Schatten S1.

proof_stages.label: Bernoulli sign embedding for the commutative criterion
proof_stages.summary: The warm-up criterion is proved by mapping a vector to the function summing its coordinates against independent signs, so basis vectors have L1 norm one while Berry-Esseen or the central limit theorem gives the sqrt(2/pi) value on smooth vectors.

proof_stages.label: Schatten-one analogue and discarded random/CAR models
proof_stages.summary: For the noncommutative proof the target space is changed from L1 to the Schatten-one trace norm, and natural candidates such as random unitaries, Gaussian random matrices, and the CAR-algebra integrality gap are analyzed and set aside.

proof_stages.label: Clifford norm identity and random-phase symmetrization
proof_stages.summary: Using Clifford generators, the speaker states an explicit Schatten-one norm formula involving the area of the parallelogram spanned by the real and imaginary parts; multiplying coordinates by random fourth roots of unity makes smooth vectors have norm about 1/sqrt(2) while preserving the norm of basis vectors, completing the required embedding.

examples.name: Diagonal unitaries as sign assignments

examples.engagement_reason: the speaker checks how diagonal unitary matrices encode sign choices and how restricting the bilinear form to diagonals reduces the noncommutative problem to the commutative one.
